vulnerabilities.title: Late gauge entrants can steal historical CREDIT rewards
vulnerabilities.summary: Reward accounting is index-based: each user should only earn deltaIndex accumulated while they had non-zero gauge weight. In practice, when a user has zero weight and re-enters a gauge, GuildToken._incrementGaugeWeight() calls ProfitManager.claimGaugeRewards(user, gauge) before the weight increment. Because current weight is zero at that moment, claimGaugeRewards() returns early without updating userGaugeProfitIndex. After weight is added, the user can claim against the full historical delta sin

description.desc: claimGaugeRewards() returns immediately when user gauge weight is zero and does not synchronize userGaugeProfitIndex; GuildToken calls this function before increasing gauge weight, leaving a stale index that lets new/returning voters claim past index growth they did not earn.

vulnerabilities.impact: Direct theft of CREDIT held by ProfitManager. Depending on available balances, attacker can drain not only guild-profit allocations but also balances backing surplus buffers, weakening or destroying first-loss capital and pushing the protocol toward insolvency/failed loss absorption.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. Honest voters keep weight on a gauge and the gauge accumulates profits via notifyPnL(), increasing gaugeProfitIndex. 2. Attacker keeps zero weight during this accrual period. 3. Attacker adds gauge weight (incrementGauge), triggering a pre-increment claim that returns early and leaves attacker index stale. 4. Attacker immediately calls claimGaugeRewards/claimRewards and receives CREDIT for historical index growth despite not voting during that time. 5. Attacker repeats by exiting before future profit events and re-entering after each event, continuously siphoning rewards.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Synchronize userGaugeProfitIndex even when current user weight is zero (set it to current gauge index baseline), or explicitly initialize index on zero->nonzero weight transitions before rewards can be claimed. Ensure increment paths cannot claim historical deltas for periods without stake.

vulnerabilities.title: LendingTerm uses user-declared collateral amounts instead of actual tokens received
vulnerabilities.summary: Collateral accounting assumes ERC20 transfers are value-preserving. In _borrow(), max borrow is computed from the declared collateralAmount and the same nominal amount is persisted in loan state, but the contract never verifies post-transfer balance delta. _addCollateral() has the same pattern. If collateral transfer delivers less than requested (transfer tax, deflationary logic, negative rebases, token-side seizure), borrowers can mint CREDIT against overstated collateral. Later loan close path

description.desc: borrow() validates collateralization and stores loan.collateralAmount from the input parameter, then performs transferFrom without checking real token inflow; fee-on-transfer/deflationary/rebasing collateral breaks accounting.

vulnerabilities.impact: Protocol insolvency and direct asset loss: unbacked CREDIT can be minted and converted to real assets, while collateral shortfall is pushed onto surplus buffers and CREDIT holders.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. A listed collateral token starts transferring less than requested (e.g., fee-on-transfer or upgraded token behavior). 2. Attacker borrows at the maximum allowed by declared collateralAmount. 3. LendingTerm receives less collateral than accounted but still mints full CREDIT to attacker. 4. Attacker exits into external value (e.g., redeeming in PSM / selling on market). 5. Repay/auction paths cannot recover the recorded collateral amount, resulting in forced bad debt socialization or forgiveness while attacker keeps extracted value.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Use balance-before/balance-after accounting for collateral transfers and base both collateral checks and stored collateralAmount on actual received tokens. Optionally hard-reject non-standard collateral behaviors (fee-on-transfer, rebasing, confiscatory tokens) at onboarding and runtime.

vulnerabilities.title: SimplePSM can over-mint CREDIT and drain peg reserves when peg token transfer amount is non-conservative
vulnerabilities.summary: PSM reserve accounting relies on exact token transfer semantics. On mint paths, pegTokenBalance is incremented by user-supplied amountIn before transferFrom and no post-transfer balance check is performed. If actual received peg tokens are lower (fee-on-transfer, transfer taxes, negative rebases between operations), the contract mints excess CREDIT relative to real reserves. Because redeem() uses internal accounting, attackers can extract disproportionately high peg token outflows from existing

description.desc: mint()/mintAndEnterRebase() increase pegTokenBalance and mint CREDIT from nominal amountIn without verifying actual peg token inflow; redeem() trusts internal pegTokenBalance for outflows.

vulnerabilities.impact: Direct reserve theft and/or peg insolvency: existing peg reserves can be siphoned and redemptions can become partially or fully unfulfillable.
vulnerabilities.proof_of_concept: 1. PSM already contains peg liquidity from honest users. 2. Attacker mints CREDIT with amountIn = X while token transfer delivers only X-f to PSM. 3. Attacker receives CREDIT as if full X was deposited. 4. Attacker redeems the minted CREDIT for peg tokens based on nominal accounting, pulling close to X out of the pool. 5. Repeating drains reserve value from existing PSM liquidity providers and eventually breaks redemptions.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Derive credited mint input from actual token balance delta (balanceAfter - balanceBefore), and maintain pegTokenBalance from measured deltas rather than user input. Add compatibility checks or explicit rejection for fee-on-transfer/rebasing peg tokens.
